title: Remotely programming the weights of a spintronic neural network by a radiofrequency broadcast signal
authors: M. Menshawy, D. Sanz-Hernández, L. Mazza, V. Puliafito, G. Finocchio, A. Jenkins, R. Ferreira, L. Benetti, J. Grollier, F. A. Mizrahi
abstract: Selectively programming large number of non-volatile synaptic weights without compromising scalability is a key challenge for in-memory computing. Here, we demonstrate remote programming of synaptic weights in series-connected chains of 11 vortex-based magnetic tunnel junctions using broadcast radiofrequency signals applied through a shared strip line. The programming relies on frequency-selective reversal of the vortex-core polarity and therefore does not require individual access lines or selector devices. By reconfiguring the binary states of these chains, we reshape the weighted sums they perform on frequency-multiplexed RF inputs. Using a 22-synapse network composed of two such chains, we remotely reconfigure the same hardware to perform two distinct tasks: handwritten-digit classification and drone RF-signature identification. The digit-optimized configuration reaches 94.91 +/- 0.26% accuracy on handwritten digits but only 13.17 +/- 0.47% on drone RF signatures, whereas the drone-optimized configuration reaches 97.33 +/- 0.62% on drones but only 47.59 +/- 1.5% on digits. Broadcast RF programming thus provides a compact and scalable route to rapidly reconfigurable spintronic neuromorphic hardware.
summary: This paper presents a way to program a spintronic neural network remotely using radiofrequency signals. By using different frequencies to target specific magnetic tunnel junctions, the researchers can change the weights of the network without needing individual wires for every synapse. This allows a single piece of hardware to be reconfigured 'on the fly' to perform different tasks, such as recognizing handwritten digits or identifying drone RF signatures. This approach offers a highly scalable and compact route for building next-generation edge intelligence hardware.
main_problem: The challenge of selectively programming a large number of non-volatile synaptic weights in dense in-memory computing architectures without the scalability bottlenecks of individual electrical access lines or complex selector devices.
main_result: Demonstrated a scalable method for remote, frequency-selective programming of 22 spintronic synapses using a single broadcast RF signal, enabling the same hardware to be reconfigured for distinct tasks like digit classification and drone signature identification.
method: The researchers used high-power RF pulses to induce frequency-selective vortex-core polarity reversal in MTJs and low-power RF signals for readout via the spin-diode effect, utilizing frequency multiplexing to address individual junctions.
model_or_system: A spintronic neural network consisting of two series-connected chains of 11 vortex-based magnetic tunnel junctions (MTJs) with varying diameters, all coupled to a shared metallic strip line for RF delivery.
key_observables: DC voltage (Vdc) across the MTJ chains, switching probability, spectral transfer function, and classification accuracy.
important_parameters: RF power (ranging from -20 to 13 dBm), frequency range (240-600 MHz), magnetic field strength (-300 to -20 mT), and MTJ diameters (300-800 nm).
main_assumptions: The model assumes the microwave field from the strip line is proportional to the RF current amplitude and neglects certain logarithmic terms in the switching field calculation.
figures_summary: Figures show the experimental setup, the frequency-dependent voltage response and spectral reshaping of the MTJ chains, the mapping of pixel intensities to RF waveforms, and the performance of the network on digit and drone identification tasks.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scalability problem in neuromorphic computing, describes the experimental MTJ-based platform and RF programming mechanism, presents the results of frequency-selective weight programming, demonstrates task-specific reconfigability, and provides an analytical scaling law for switching energy.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Schematic of a perceptron and equivalent circuit of a row of selector-controlled memory devices. (b) Schematic of a chain of spintronic synapses programmed by a broadcast RF signal. (c) Reading the synaptic state of a single MTJ: dc voltage Vdc across an MTJ versus RF input frequency, at a power of -20 dBm and at zero field. (d) Programming the synapse: switching probability versus RF input frequency, at a power of 0 dBm. The red and blue curves respectively represent switching from down to up and up to down. The red, grey and blue shaded zones respectively represent a final state that is up, random or down. Probabilities are evaluated over 10 independent trials. (f) Response...

Figure 2. (a-f) Response of the chain versus input frequency. The response is the voltage difference between the measured configuration and the reference (fully down) state. Each panel corresponds to a different configuration. “1” and “0” respectively refer to up and down states. (g) Density map of the chain response over all 2048 configurations. For each input frequency, the color scale indicates how many configurations produce a given output voltage. By applying sequences of RF write pulses selected from the programming bands identified in Figure 1f, we can set each 11-junction chain into any of its 211 = 2048 binary configurations (Methods). Starting from a fully down or fully up...

Figure 3. (a) Schematic of an image sent to the network as an RF signal. (b) Examples of spectra for images of the digits dataset. (c) Examples of spectra of the drones signatures. (d-g) Histograms of the voltage difference between the response of chains 0 and 1, for the different datasets and network configurations. The dashed vertical line corresponds to a difference of 0, which separates the histograms when classification is perfect. Here the network comprises only 22 binary synapses. Thus, we can evaluate, for all possible network configurations, the classification accuracy over the whole dataset, defined as the proportion of correctly classified examples.
